If you call `Timer::FireAt` with a time that's in the past (`< now` as compared in the timer code) then the alarm is set to 0 msec and the timer tasklet will be posted immediately. See the code here: https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/main/src/core/common/timer.cpp#L173. So there's no harm in calling `GetEarliestActiveTimer()` even if there are expired timers, since you will choose an expired timer, set  `mTimer` to fire at its expired time in the past, causing the alarm to be set to 0 msec, and then call those callbacks when the Timer callback occurs.

This was in the previous implementation of the runtimes handling (see [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/appservice/custom.py#L2896)). Windows runtimes are not set with a single attribute like linux runtimes (which uses `linuxFxVersion`), so we have to set them with the proper appsetting key and value. For function apps, the API tells you what appsettings to set, but the webapps API does not  

This is used only for `az webapp up` and `az webapp create`, so if the user deletes the appsetting after the creation of the webapp it will not impact either command.

As far as error handling, the appsetting is only set if the runtime has an entry in the config mapping, so there's no exception if a runtime doesn't have a value in the dict.

- Also If I remember correctly in C++11 we can only forward-declare an `enum` with a specified underlying (integral) type/size (or if it is a `enum class` and not `enum`).

`Client()` constructor already takes `opener: Optional[urllib.request.OpenerDirector]`, and [`OpenerDirector`] allows to override its default `User-Agent` header.  According to the [docs][1]:

> [`OpenerDirector`] automatically adds a `User-Agent` header to every [`Request`]. To change this:
>
> ```python
> import urllib.request
> opener = urllib.request.build_opener()
> opener.addheaders = [('User-agent', 'Mozilla/5.0')]
> opener.open('http://www.example.com/')
> ```

Therefore, IMHO it'd be better to check if it's using the default opener or a passed custom `opener`:

```suggestion
        if self._using_default_opener:
```

[1]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.request.html#examples

For NCCL, we need to avoid setting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`.  NCCL needs to have visibility of adjacent devices in order to use CUDA IPC.  Typically, we handle GPU isolation at the framework level, for example:

```
torch.cuda.set_device(hvd.local_rank())
```

If you need to set `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` because multiple Ray applications may be using the same nodes, then we can set `"CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES": gpu_ids` so every worker can see the devices in its local communicator.

**I'm okay with "slope" azimuth & zenith instead of "system"** b/c in practice, I believe most if not all systems will be orientated parallel to the slope. Also in the NREL tech report we call this plane the "grade slope" with suffix "g" - so for consistency, perhaps we should use the same terminology, _ie_ "slope". **I'm also okay with "tilt" instead of "zenith".** -- @kanderso-nrel, care to comment?

As far as the purpose of `calc_axis_tilt()` -- `axis_tilt` is a required input for `singleaxis()` but it's not intuitively obvious to me what it should be for a system on a slope that was not parallel to the tracker axis azimuth. Consider the system in the image below:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1385621/92173094-c74f0500-edf0-11ea-9c55-2fcfb5793b6f.png)

(@kanderso-nrel pls correct me if I'm inconsistent, thx)

The `axis_slope` is β<sub>a</sub> while the slope (or system) tilt is β<sub>g</sub>. The `side_slope` is β<sub>c</sub> where the "c" stands for "cross-axis", _ie_ to the "side" perpendicular to the tracker axis. The slope azimuth (CD) is not parallel to the tracker axis azimuth BD, so given only the slope (or system) orientation, we would use `calc_axis_tilt()` to get the required input for `singleaxis`. Also because the tracker rows (thin black lines) are constrained to the slope (the grey plane), the `side_slope` is also required by `singleaxis()` or else the trackers will shade each other as indicated in Kevin's paper: **[Maximizing Yield with Improved Single-Axis
Backtracking on Cross-Axis Slopes](https://www.nrel.gov/docs/fy20osti/76023.pdf)**.

It should transparently pass the `message` argument as the part of `args` passed to the super consturctor (See also: [`args` of `BaseException`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#BaseException.args), [PEP-352](https://peps.python.org/pep-0352/)).
[Stripping arguments for the super constructor affects *picklability* of exceptions.](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/16244923/how-to-make-a-custom-exception-class-with-multiple-init-args-pickleable)  If exceptions are not picklable, it will make it hard to read the stderr exception logs when the service uses `ai.backend.common.logging` with a multi-process architecture.

```python
class WekaAPIError(WekaError):
    message: str

    def __init__(self, message: Optional[str] = None, *args):
        # Unless we explicitly utilize keyword arguments,
        # there is no need to pass them to the super constructor.
        super().__init__(message, *args)
        # just additionally define typed attributes
        self.message = message or ""
```

`Any` basically means dynamic typing, i.e. we don't know if it will work or not.

Alternatively, you could even define it with:

class EllipticCurve(Protocol):

The root cause of the CredentialsMissingError seems to be that after a `_reset_protocol` the credentials class inside libcoap forgets the credentials [0][1]. Users see this problem occur after a network timeout / connection reset.
To resolve this, we pro-actively update the coap credentials.
If we wouldn't, we'd throw the CredentialsMissingError exception after immediately resetting the protocol, reset the protocol again and then only fix the credentials.

[0] https://github.com/chrysn/aiocoap/blob/master/aiocoap/credentials.py#L277
[1] https://github.com/home-assistant/core/issues/42563#issuecomment-725520545

We are in complete agreement that we shouldn't use `NodeProto.name` to store the `GraphProto.name`. I will try to exand on my original comment.
> By using the name as the graph identifier, you will only be able to have one instance, since node names should be unique. I think it would be better to use an attribute, e.g. graph_name.

### Store graph name in NodeProto.op_type
Using `NodeProto.op_type` would look like this:
```python
onnx.helper.make_node(
    op_type = model.graph.name,
    domain = "",
    name = "any_valid_c_identifier_for_diagnostic_purposes_only",
    inputs = ["a", "b", "c"],
    outputs = ["x", "y", "z"],
)
```
That conflates names from the `Graph` namespace (`GraphProto.name`) and the `Operator` namespace (`NodeProto.op_type`). cref https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/IR.md#names-within-a-graph

Users seeing the op_type in error messages, performance logs, etc. or just trying to learn onnx will probably be confused that they can't find the operator in the Operators.md list.

Additionally, as you have pointed out, graphs cannot use names from the default operator set, and new releases of the ai.onnx operator set could add names which break existing models and graphs.

### Store the graph name in an attribute
I am suggesting an alternative: I think a `Graph` operator is a much clearer mechanism:
```python
onnx.helper.make_node(
    op_type = "Graph",
    domain = "",
    name = "any_valid_c_identifier_for_diagnostic_purposes_only",
    inputs = ["a", "b", "c"],
    outputs = ["x", "y", "z"],
    # Attributes:
    graph_name = model.graph.name,
)
```

The `Graph` operator can be listed in the Operators.md document, with clearly described semantics and limitations (e.g. currently only usable in training info to refer to the ModelProto.graph inference graph). It would be a special operator requiring runtime support similarly to how the `Gradient` operator requires runtime support.

Since the copy of the attrs was a shallow copy (normal `dict.copy()`) the alpha enhancement history was adding an extra `(255, 0)` scale/offset entry causing one test to fail.

See my comment above, I think this happens if `gemm` is passed arrays of different types. Things like

```
dX = slope * ((low < X) & (X < high))
```

Are not safe, since they discard the data type of X and result in an array that has the same data type as `slope`. E.g., if `slope` is a Python `float`, this fill create a `np.float64` array. However, this is incorrect when the input as a `np.float32` array. Then in the caller of the backward function, a `gemm` is attempted of an `np.float32` and an `np.float64` array.

See my earlier comment for an approach (with `np.where`) that should not have this issue.

Also, we need to be careful here with doing simple if checks for appending to the hash. What if we had 2 different parameters, x and y that are part of the hash with logic like this:

```python
    if x:
        hash_input.append(x)
    if y:
        hash_input.append(y)
```

Then I have a template where I set x to 'foo' and don't set y. Then later, I remove the x property and set y to 'foo'. The deployment hash will be the same even though I've made an actual change to my template. We should be building this array such that we avoid this problem. For example, we could avoid the above scenario by doing this:

```python
    if x:
        hash_input.append('x:{}'.format(x))
    if y:
        hash_input.append('y:{}'.format(y))
```

Whatever we do, we need to make it backwards-compatible with the current behavior, but moving forward, we should do something like this with new values that we decide to add to the hash array.

OK Here is another approach that confines this to JSONResponse

```
class JSONResponse(Response):
    media_type = "application/json"
    encoder = None

    def render(self, content: typing.Any) -> bytes:
        return json.dumps(
            content,
            ensure_ascii=False,
            allow_nan=False,
            indent=None,
            separators=(",", ":"),
            default=self.encoder
        ).encode("utf-8")
```

We now have to also add 'self' to our encoder method

```
def utf8encoder(self, val):
    if isinstance(val, bytes):
        return val.decode('utf-8')
```

And then we can use it like this...

```
JSONResponse.encoder = utf8encoder
return JSONResponse({'params': params, '__dict__': request.__dict__})
```

Things I like about this approach: 

- Functionality is there
- It's contained to JSONResponse

Things I don't like with this approach.

- The encoder has to have 'self' making it no longer a generic encoder that works with vanilla json.dumps.  Would be nice to have a library of encoders that work with both.
- The encoder could be set at module scope reducing the calling signature for each Response call.

I think the approach that will feel the most naturally as a developer using the framework will be to override the JSONResponse.__init__.  That way it could be fully contained in JSONResponse, but since Response.__init__ has all named parameters this means overriding the entire signature which doesn't seem like a great approach either... 

@dmontagu What's your opinion on the above?  I'd certainly rather have the first approach outlined than not have the ability to pass the encoder at all.  The second approach unless I'm missing something obvious doesn't feel solid either.

I think you may need to elaborate on this, but v1 does intend to support this. Essentially, the breakdown of the different usages of this tool include:

- `detect-secrets scan`: scans secrets, and adds new secrets to a baseline
- `detect-secrets-hook`: blocks new secrets
- `detect-secrets audit`: analyzes results

In v1, scan does take in a baseline (e.g. `detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline`), but since scan's job is to create a baseline, it uses this baseline as a configuration file, and updates it with the latest secrets. The only assumptions in the pre-commit hook (as written) is:

- new secrets should be blocked
- people should be able to see which new secrets were blocked
- baselines should be regularly updated (which can be reduced with a WIP PR for removal of line numbers)

The hook should not be dependent on the `pre-commit` framework, which I intend to address via better documentation.

I'll address each of your points:

1. The `rewriteToken` is not a retry operation, but a mechanic exposed by GCP to handle large copies. If the `rewriteTo` operation is incomplete, GCP requests that we keep following the API chain until it is complete. In that sense, it doesn't throw and we don't have to use a `try-except` block here.
2. To the best of my knowledge, exceptions in the event loop as a whole don't stop other coroutines from running unless the exception is not caught and crashes the program. In that case, the `rewriteTo` operation will expire in 7 days if not resumed and we're back to our initial state, so there's nothing to worry about. If the call is made again, then to the best of my knowledge a new `rewriteTo` operation is created and again after 7 days the previously unfinished one expired.

@DaelonSuzuka 

> I originally tried to write this section without launching a new interpreter at all

Yeah, I figured this would be necessarily due to the complexity and sensitivity of the QtPy startup and Qt import process, so I didn't question your decision here.

> `sys.executable` is new to me, thanks.

I've had to interact with it all the time, to help users debug issues and set interpreter paths, as well as programmatically for various things with Spyder and elsewhere, so I guess I just assumed that an experianced Python like yourself would be aware of it, sorry.

> Do you have any recommended reading on launching python from python? I'm sure this isn't the last time I'll need to do this.

For launching a brand new interpreter with no connection to the previous, aside from possibly env variables and CLI args. it just boils down to `subprcoess.run([sys.executable, *python_args_here], **subprocess_kwargs_here)`. That's how we do it in Spyder for, e.g. LSP and environment detection. Per the [admonition in the subprocess docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen),

> On all platforms, passing [sys.executable](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.executable) is the recommended way to launch the current Python interpreter again, and use the -m command-line format to launch an installed module.

If you want/need something more tightly coupled, where the processes can exchange data (IPC) or even share state, there's too many options to describe—threading, multiprocessing, concurrent.futures, asyncio, third party async frameworks, subinterpreters, third-party IPC systems like ZMQ (as we use with Spyder/Spyder-Kernels) and RabbitMQ, OS-level mechanisms like pipes and green threads, etc, etc...but for just the case of launching a brand new, independent Python interpreter, it basically just boils down to the line above, with whatever CLI args, env vars and other setup you want.

output = func(...)

func(..., output=output)

Other libraries may wish to use the "bad syntax" to support users of either camp, but we shouldn't encourage end users to do so.

The conventions have changed through time.   I can't locate the old sun docs which irc were more explicit.  The general rule is that underscore and dollar are valid names for packages, variables, and classes, but dollar should not be used (compiler reserved) and underscore should only be used if the next character is not a valid character for starting an identifier.  So ``_2cool`` is fine because 2 can't start an identifier.   Double underscore  would fail to follow that convention and thus be discouraged.   Of course starting names with underscore is also reserved in C (for compiler and linker symbols), but since there is nothing preventing it you will find plenty of code with it. 

 https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/nutsandbolts/variables.html

Not only is the `await Get` heavy, but adding a rule for every single `Field` is extremely heavy-weight, both with boilerplate and with blowing up the rule graph. For example, we'd need to introduce a `Result` class for each of the `PrimitiveField`s now so that the graph has a product - we couldn't use a generic wrapper like `FieldResult` because then MyPy couldn't infer the underlying type, and 100% MyPy support is a crucial requirement of this project. 

Compare:

```python
compatibility: List[str] = my_tgt.get(Compatibility).value
```

vs.

```python
compatibility_result = await Get[CompatibilityResult](Compatibility, my_tgt.get(Compatiblity)
compatibility: List[str] = compatibility_result.value
```

Hm, apparently not, `flake8-annotations` doesn't seem to be capable of detecting `Any` in nested  types, so say `list[Any]` would not be a violation of ANN401. The docs mention this: https://github.com/sco1/flake8-annotations#dynamic-typing-caveats

Additionally, variables (including class variables) aren't enforced to not contain `Any` at all, `flake8-annotations` only checks `Any` in functions (returns or arguments).

Ruff replicates this behavior and also doesn't consider `Any` in nested types to be an issue.

However I have also noticed that we actually don't seem to even have `ANN401` enabled. This is an optionally enforced rule, and needs to be selected (`flake8 --extend-select=ANN401`), but if you take a look at the `.flake8` config file (now removed) didn't contain `extend-select=ANN401`, so apparently we weren't even enforcing this in the first place.

I suppose the noqas were added manually, because of the use of Any was suspected to be an issue, without actually checking? I'm not quite sure. However I did put together this minimal example, to show how `flake8-annotations` behaves on various things, and indeed it only reports issues when `Any` is used directly, and only as a function return/argument annotation:

<details>
<summary>Example code</summary>

```python
from typing import Any, ClassVar, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")

a: Any = 5
b: list[Any] = [1, "a", 3.5]


class Foo:
    X: ClassVar[Any] = 10
    Y: Any = 50


def func1(x: list[Any]) -> object:
    ...


def func2(x: Any) -> object:
    ...


def func3(x: object) -> Any:
    ...


def func4(x: object) -> list[Any]:
    ...
```

With just flake8 and flake8-annotations installed, running `flake8 --extend-select=ANN401 --show-source main.py` shows this output:

```
main.py:18:11: ANN401 Dynamically typed expressions (typing.Any) are disallowed
def func2(x: Any) -> object:
          ^
main.py:22:28: ANN401 Dynamically typed expressions (typing.Any) are disallowed
def func3(x: object) -> Any:
                           ^
```

</details>

Even though LightGBM code would look the same, these types of things would not be caught if we don't have a build with Visual Studio + R 3.6.x:

* a linker flag is added to `CMakeLists.txt` in an `if(MSVC)` block that works when linking to the `R.lib` for R 4.0 but not to the `R.lib` for R 3.6.3
* C++ code is added in a block guarded by `#ifdef _MSC_VER` which uses a function that R prohibits (like `abort`), or which conflicts with some other symbol being read in from `R.h` or the other R headers

Basically the existence of `if(MSVC)` in `CMakeLists.txt` and `#ifdef _MSCV_VER` in our C++ code opens the possibility of something being incompatible between a `LightGBM` compiled with MSVC and R 3.6.3.

We know as of this moment that there are no such issues because we have a CI build that tests that combination. If we're very worried about CI time, I am ok dropping the build that is "Visual Studio + R 3.6.3" because of that, but it does mean we're losing some coverage of the issues I mentioned above.

Just noting that descriptions like this one, with lists embedded, appear in the pre-existing `sbp.pdf` as a single paragraph of unformatted text.  Likewise with the comments in Haskell.  That is obviously not what is intended, however there are not many in the yaml, and the resulting rendering is still usable, so this PR is not going to try to fix that.

In C/Python/Java at least, the EOL are preserved, but additional lines are not aligned with indent of the first line.

Changing these from `|` string blocks to `>` string blocks does not cause them to become a single paragraph in the C/Java/etc - the indented lines are still treated as pre-formatted.

This patch will fix the alignment of them in C/Python/Java, however it cant autowrap the content inside the bullet points - they still need to be pre-formatted in the YAML.

That's a good point. I started by thinking that rename_query would take in a `RenamedSchemaDescriptor`, but moved away from that idea for several reasons, but there are definitely arguments on both sides.

Taking in a `RenamedSchemaDescriptor` means that rename_query is probably being used in conjunction with rename_schema as expected, and it also allows validity checking (if necessary) since we have access to its schema.

One issue though is that I think users may be confused about which direction rename_query operates, if the input is a `RenamedSchemaDescriptor`. Suppose the user previous renamed schema S to S'. The intended use of rename_query will change query Q' written in terms of types in S' into a query Q written in terms of types in S. However, a user may think that rename_query instead changes Q to Q', in the same direction as rename_schema. Passing in a renamings dictionary doesn't cause this kind of confusion.

    def wait_for(func, func_args=[], func_kwargs={}, logger=None, **kwargs):
        """Waits for a certain amount of time for an action to complete
        Designed to wait for a certain length of time,
        either linearly in 1 second steps, or exponentially, up to a maximum.
        Returns the output from the function once it completes successfully,
        along with the time taken to complete the command.
        Note: If using the expo keyword, the returned elapsed time will be inaccurate
            as wait_for does not know the exact time that the function returned
            correctly, only that it returned correctly at last check.
        Args:
            func: A function to be run
            func_args: A list of function arguments to be passed to func
            func_kwargs: A dict of function keyword arguments to be passed to func
            num_sec: An int describing the number of seconds to wait before timing out.
            timeout: Either an int describing the number of seconds to wait before timing out. Or a
                :py:class:`timedelta` object. Or a string formatted like ``1h 10m 5s``. This then sets
                the ``num_sec`` variable.
            expo: A boolean flag toggling exponential delay growth.
            message: A string containing a description of func's operation. If None,
                defaults to the function's name.
            fail_condition: An object describing the failure condition that should be tested
                against the output of func. If func() == fail_condition, wait_for continues
                to wait. Can be a callable which takes the result and returns boolean whether to fail.
                You can also specify it as a  set, that way it checks whether it is present in the
                iterable.
            handle_exception: A boolean controlling the handling of excepetions during func()
                invocation. If set to True, in cases where func() results in an exception,
                clobber the exception and treat it as a fail_condition.
            delay: An integer describing the number of seconds to delay before trying func()
                again.
            fail_func: A function to be run after every unsuccessful attempt to run func()
            quiet: Do not write time report to the log (default False)
            very_quiet: Do not log unless there was an error (default False). Implies quiet.
            silent_failure: Even if the entire attempt times out, don't throw a exception.
        Returns:
            A tuple containing the output from func() and a float detailing the total wait time.
        Raises:
            TimedOutError: If num_sec is exceeded after an unsuccessful func() invocation.

> Is it the case that all resources have at least one property? If so, this is ok. Otherwise, say you had a resource with no props, and you moved to a version where props were added. It feels like we would then say "we should use the news, instead of sticking with the empty olds.

We recently began reflecting the input properties as the output properties for all provider resources. As a result, the only way a provider resource can have no output properties in the checkpoint is if it had no input properties when it was created, or if it was created prior to the changes that began reflecting inputs to outputs.

In the former case, both old inputs and old outputs will be empty, so there will be no semantic difference with these changes.

In the latter case, the old inputs will not be empty, but the old state will be empty. This can cause unexpected diffs when dealing with providers that implement `DiffConfig` (e.g. the k8s provider): because these providers are presented an empty old state, they are very likely to see a difference between the empty old state and the current inputs, even if the user's program has not changed. Reflecting the old inputs into the new state is logically performing an upgrade from the older format to the newer format.

Consider the scenario where a user is on https://taskcluster-web.netlify.com/secrets/garbage%2Farmenzg%2FfakeOrg.

Using `clearStore`:
* User is able to see the secret.
* User logs out.
* `clearStore` will cause the store to be cleared.
* The user still sees the secret.

Using `resetStore`:
* User is able to see the secret.
* User logs out.
* `resetStore` will cause the store to be cleared and all active queries to be refetched.
* User sees an error saying they are missing permissions to view the secret. This will work for the secrets view but will fail for the TaskGroup since all of the `fetchMore` queries won't be triggered therefore showing only the first 20 tasks https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/blob/f283feaacb60b06d47c2a24344e857f9a2c7d217/ui/src/views/Tasks/TaskGroup/index.jsx#L52

Using the `key` prop and the `network-only` policy:
* User is able to see the secret.
* User logs out.
* The `key` prop will change. This will reconstruct the view from scratch (i.e., the constructor will run again; this is different than just re-running the render method). Changing the key will have the same effect as reloading the page but in this case the page is the view component.

* The graphql query of that view will see that it has `network-only` as a policy so it will always make a request to the server. The response will overwrite whatever was in the store for that query.
* User sees an error saying they are missing permissions to view the secret.

Right now on master Black enforces this formatting:
```pyi
class A: ...

@bar
class B: ...
    def BMethod(self) -> Any: ...

class C: ...
```

While the empty line between class B and C is right, I am not sure if the empty line between class A and class B is right since class A has an empty body. That empty line happens since Black can't tell the difference between the previous class not having an empty body or the current class being preceded by a decorator. It doesn't look like that's happened yet, so I won't merge right away. But for generality of utility functions, note that the three cases all do different things:

   * NumpyArray has a raw buffer of bytes; how it should be divided up into threads and blocks depends on if and how those bytes are being interpreted. Reducers interpret the bytes as numbers, but basic copy operations like `carry` only need to know byte ranges. NumpyArrays might not be contiguous: `array[:, :-1]` is an example of an operation that takes a contiguous `array` to a non-contiguous one. For some operations, dealing with non-contiguousness was too hard to think about so we run `NumpyArray::contiguous` to get it into that state before calling the kernel, but others deal with the non-contiguousness via `strides` and `shape`.
   * Index always has a compile-time integer type, is always one-dimensional, and is always contiguous. We use Indexes a lot more than NumpyArray (for example, `ak.num` doesn't need to see the underlying NumpyArray: the number of items is described by a list node's `offsets`, `starts`/`stops`, or `size`). Since we use them everywhere and don't want to overcomplicate the code, Index is forced to be contiguous.
   * Identities always has a compile-time integer type, is always two-dimensional, and is always contiguous. We also don't use them much.

Even though one of these (Identities) is two-dimensional, and some NumpyArray data are n-dimensional (if `len(shape) > 1`), I don't think we ever need to use CUDA's two or three-dimensional blocks because we never make use of n-dimensional "neighbor-ness" in any kernels. I think the two and three-dimensional blocks exist for people who are doing image analysis or PDE solving, in which memory for cell `(i, j)` needs to be close to `(i + 1, j)` and `(i, j + 1)` because they're taking differences or computing convolutions across those dimensions. Some of our (non-embarrassingly parallel) kernels need to relate cell `i` with `i + 1`, but never anything that resembles a two or three-dimensional grid.

Just in case you're thinking that we might need to do that—we don't.

> This makes the default docker run reasonable, makes it easy to run pulumi commands, but (unlike ENTRYPOINT) doesn't constrain use of the image for other scenarios,

I'm not actually sure this is the case.  When we use `CMD` it's the default command that docker will run if you don't pass any arguments to `docker run`.  But simply running `pulumi` is uninteresting. It will simply print help and exit.

If you want to actually do anything interesting, you have to pass a command to `docker run`, and here `CMD` means you'd need to pass a full command line, not just arguments that are appended to pulumi.

Both `node` and `python` can use CMD here because they have repls and its reasonable to drop you into them by default.  We have no such analog.

This leads me to a question about the intended use of the public `registered` method.

The documentation says:
> Registered responses are available as a public method of the RequestMock instance. It is sometimes useful for debugging purposes to view the stack of registered responses which can be accessed via responses.registered().

Currently, the `registered` method gives direct access to the **mutable private attribute** `_matches`, which allows direct manipulation of the internal state of the `RequestsMock` instance. I know that there is not a list view object in python, but from the documentation, it appears to me like the user should only get a view by this method and manipulate the registry with the `add`, `replace`, `upsert`, and `remove` methods.
Such a view (even though static compared to `dict.values()` for instance) could be implemented by returning `_matches.copy()`, or `[response for index, response in self._matching_sequence()]`. I would vote for the second one, because as said I think matching order is more relevant to the user. I would also say that it is most backward compatible for the debugging use-case mentioned in the documentation, meaning that users would like to know why another match had been picked instead of the one they expected, so they want to see the matching order before and after doing their request.
Now the question: do you see any conflict or critical backward incompatibility by changing the return value of the `registered` method?

The problem is that `from_api_repr()` is defined on the base `_JobConfig` class and its return type is annotated as `_JobConfig`. Which makes sense on the base class, but in practice the actual `cls` subclass is chosen here dynamically by introspecting `job_config`, and `mypy` cannot understand that.

Specifying the type of `load_job_config` at assignment time would not help, because again a base type (`_JobConfig`) is assigned to `load_job_config`  with a specific sub-type, and `mypy` would still complain. Cast solves this.

The alternative could be changing the return type on the base class to include all sub-classes, but that doesn't feel right. It can get of of sync if a new sub-type is created, and a circular import would be needed.
Besides, `mypy` would probably _still_ complain if `"Union[All, Config, Subtypes]"` is assigned to a _specific_ sub-class type.

I see two threads here. 

First, about _this_ implementation throwing an exception when `inject_username` is `False`. Having mulled it over a bit more, I think logging a warning and returning `self.find` is probably a better UX. For example, a user that has a username that is valid for a deployment to a packaging org who wants to replace it when deploying to a scratch org could have `inject_username: True` on some flows but not others.

---

Second, about the intuitiveness of `patterns`:

>  In this case we know how to retrieve the desired data to replace the find value with (i.e. org_config.username) so the user should only have to specify a find token.

That's a great point, Brandon. Ultimately there are two classes of substitutions:

1. Arbitrary user-defined strings (`'foo' -> 'bar'`)
2. Known metadata with a token we define (`%%%NAMESPACE%%%`)

Username and ID fall into the second category, IMO. In other words, we'd define a token and replace that (`$USERNAME$ -> cae8ou@example.com`). Being able to use the token in arbitrary substitutions would be a nice bonus:

```yaml
patterns:
  - find: username@production.org
    replace: $USERNAME$
```

We could define tokens for other OrgConfig values (org_id, etc) too.

I have been checking the redis-cli code.

When the Redis client is initialized without arguments, as we do, it creates a connection pool for that client instance. It is done here:
https://github.com/redis/redis-py/blob/3a121bef7bbc5bb5f07b119b0eef2f7527a38eda/redis/client.py#L1033

Then each time a command is executed it uses the current connection (if there is one active) or it gets one from the connection pool:
```
conn = self.connection or pool.get_connection(command_name, **options)
```
https://github.com/redis/redis-py/blob/3a121bef7bbc5bb5f07b119b0eef2f7527a38eda/redis/client.py#L1251

The documentation mentions that in case of using single_connection_client then no connection pool is used and it is not thread safe:
```
single_connection_client:
            if `True`, connection pool is not used. In that case `Redis`
            instance use is not thread safe.
```

If a new process is forked then it will have to instantiate its own TokenRedis and it will have its own connection pool, so you are right at the end in each forked process there will be a new connection pool. The advantage is that if this process does several gets then they will make use of the pool.

Yes, this makes sense. I've had a read over the scikit-learn codebase and it seems they've got a mixture of checks - some upon `__init__`, but _mostly_ upon `fit`.
For reference, some of the exceptions where checks are performed upon `__init__`:
- [decomposition/_kernel_pca.py](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/decomposition/_kernel_pca.py#L151) (commit from 2012-02-17, 9c6e0445ae)
- [decomposition/_fastica.py](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/decomposition/_fastica.py#L400) (commit from 2018-04-04, 1a3201cc30)
- [feature_extraction/text.py](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/feature_extraction/text.py#L1027) (commit from 2013-03-14, 3444bfbe88)
- [svm/_base.py](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/svm/_base.py#L81) (commit from 2020-02-16, 9b39c4c4d2)

Seems it's not necessarily been a recent guideline in their API, but I confirm I'm happy to update this PR accordingly.

P.S.: It seems the the scikit-learn codebase is not very consistent between `_check_params` and `_check_parameters` either. By having `IntegrationConfig` inherit `AttrDict`/`dict` we run into issues with having attributes that we want to be private/exclusive to the `IntegrationConfig` itself. This results in some nastiness in `__getattribute__`, `__getitem__` and `__setattr__`. And of course we have to be sure to keep this set up to date with whatever future attributes may be added to the `IntegrationConfig`.

To me it feels like we could solve this by having an `AttrDict` attribute within the `IntegrationConfig` that we use in `__getattribute__`, `__getitem__` and `__setattr__`. The usage of the `IntegrationConfig` would stay the same, but the implementation would be a lot cleaner, and I don't _think_ we sacrifice anything in the process. Thoughts on this design choice?

@tacaswell and I now consider `configure` to be a misfeature. It is similar to `set` (or a bunch of `set`s) with the side-effect of notifying the RunEngine, "Any Event Descriptor documents you emitted with these configuration readers are now out of date, so if/when you emit new readings from this device, emit a fresh Event Descriptor as well."

The major problem with this is that the configuration setting could be changed _externally_. (Example: Someone runs `caput` or pokes a button in CSStudio.) In that way, the Event Descriptor would become "wrong" but the RunEngine would never know. The proper way to handle this is to let ophyd _notify_ the RunEngine that some signal that was dubbed "configuration" has changed under it, and that it should reissue an Event Descriptor.

We have known about this problem for awhile (maybe as far back as 2016) but it has never been a priority because `configure` is extremely rarely used. Let's take this moment to formally deprecate it, and drop this class entirely from the PR.

In fact, because it is so rarely used, we discussed going as far as immediately repurposing the method name along the lines proposed by @thomascobb elsewhere in the conversation on this PR.

A standard flow is as following:
1. InteractiveFlow: interactive questionnaire that gather the user's answers and create the cookiecutter context 
2. Preprocessing: a template-defined logic to process the context, for example the preprocessing of the pipeline template(coming next) is to create the required AWS resources if the user didn't provide it
3. Cookiecutter: generating the cookiecutter project form the template's files
4. postprocessing: a template-defined logic. for example the pipeline templates will use the post-processing to print instructions on what resources it created on behalf of the users.

So a template defines an interactive-flow, pre-processors and post-processors.

Now, multiple templates may have a similar flow and processing logic, and this is where the **plugin** concept come into place. We can extract the common interactive-flow, preprocessor and postprocessor to a plugin, then plug it into each of these template which will just append these three components to the corresponding template's components.

Regarding the order; it will be the template's own component then the plugins' components in the same order they are plugged in.

Example:
t = Template(interactive_flows=[f1, f2], preprocessors=[p1, p2,], postprocessors=[p3, p4], plugins=[g1,g2])
the order of execution of each component will be as following:
1. interactive_flows: f1, f2, g1.interactive_flow, g2.interactive_flow
2. preprocesspors: p1, p2, g1.preprocesspor, g2.preprocesspor
3. postprocesspors: p3, p4, g1.postprocesspor, g2.postprocesspor

> But couldn't it return an entire non-mapping mapping from module -> target, with multiple entries per module? Then the consuming code would group by module and report all of the targets?

This is already what is happening. Each specific implementation returns a `FrozenDict[str, tuple[Address, ...]]`. There can be multiple entries per module if that makes sense within the context of that implementation, e.g. the handling of `.pyi` files. But within the vast majority of implementations, it is not valid to have >1 target per module, and it's ambiguous.

> Then the consuming code would group by module and report all of the targets?

This does happen, although we assume that if the same module is covered by >1 implementation, it must be ambiguous. There's no use case yet to allow a module to have multiple owners, each coming from a different implementation.

Let me add another idea/suggestion here to get people's thoughts on this.

I noticed we use `otInstance *` and `otTcpEndPoint *` as the paramters for the APIs and callbacks.

We can consider changing this as follows:
- We can assume a `otTcpEndPoint` would remember the `otInstance` it belongs to (it remembers it from a  successfully initialization from `otTcpEndpointInitialize()`.
- Then in all other APIs/callbacks we just use `otTcpEndPoint *` (and not pass the `otInstance *` anymore)
- We can also provide a new API for application to get the associated instance with an initialized `otTcpEndPoint`, something like:
```
otInstance *otTcpEndPointGetInstance(otTcpEndpoint *aEndpoint);
```
---------

I am myself somewhat on the fence on this:
- On one hand, we do use `otInstance *` as the first argument in almost all existing APIs (including UDP socket), so it may be good to keep it harmonized and same.
- On the other hand, I see the simplification that comes (ease of use) by just passing a `otTcpEndpoint *`.

After thinking more about this, I think I agreed with you about several things:

1. `And then, wouldn't it make more sense to map the target_prefix_ids to the reduced vocabulary space right away before going into the beam search loop? vocab_slice_ids is fixed for a single request, so the 'reduced' vocabulary space is the same for each beam search iteration (which is why we cache the reduction operation in the OutputLayer).
Similarly, we could also one-hot-encode all time steps of the target prefix once before the loop, inf-mask it, and then only pull out the slice for each time step here. This would reduce memory allocations in each loop and be likely faster.` -> It is true that we should map the target_prefix_ids to the reduced vocabulary space right away before going into the beam search loop. This is a very obvious thing I should do and somehow I missed it indeed.

2. `You can do that with x = pt.full(<shape>, value=np.inf); x[:, target_prefix_ids] = 0 Within the beam search loop, your logic would then simplify to something like scores += x.` I agreed that while mask_filled_ works perfectly fine, it is better here to change it as simple as `scores += x`. It is better because of at least two reasons (1): it looks simpler indeed. and (2): it is consistent because it follows our practice of similar stuff (+ first_step_mask, + scores in _update_scores). Furthermore, the logic here can be applied exactly to prefix factors masking. So yeah I like this idea much more now. So I changed our code based on your suggestion.

BTW Regarding (2): I also changed the generate_one_hot_function to `gen_prefix_masking` instead, as we now create a new matrix which are inf everywhere except zero for prefix ids. But the logic is still the same for that function (i.e. gen_prefix_masking) because it is based on the pt.nn.functional.one_hot. So everything is ``smooth" here.

Meanwhile I still prefer keeping +x separately instead of putting it into UpdateScores at the moment. There are two reasons here. 1. In greedy module we don't have `updatescores` stuff. So keeping the same line of code (+x) between two modules makes things consistency. 2. tracing `UpdateScores` seems does not gain if not hurt on GPUs so at least there is no need yet to add stuff into UpdateScores at the moment for a better performance.

One high level comment I want to make here re: my personal testing philosophy is about the impossibility of testing all cases.
If we have 20 config parameters for `sentry.init` each taking 2 values (boolean), all possible combinations of those are `2^20 =1048576` total cases. Even more if those are not booleans. If any of those values is continuous, theoretically you need an infinite number of tests to test every possible state of your program/api surface.
Given this theoretical state of affairs, as _engineers_, we need to pick and choose where we write tests and not all tests add value/are worth adding.

I see. You want both `hub.start_transaction("txn-name")` and `hub.start_transaction(Transaction.continue_from_headers(headers))` to be usable as the first positional argument.

This makes me think again why a `Transaction` cannot start itself.

There is a lack of symmetry in that `Span` and `Transaction` have a `.finish()` method, but no `.start()` method, and need to be started by passing them to another object.

The alternative would write as:

```py
with Transaction(name="txn-name"):     # Transaction is a context manager, __enter__ => start, __exit__ => finish
    pass

with Transaction.continue_from_headers(headers):     # continue_from_headers returns a new Transaction
    pass
```

And if you need access to the transaction within the block, e.g. to set the name later:

```py
with Transaction() as transaction:
    job = queue.get()
    transaction.name = f"worker-job-{job.name}"
    job.run()
```

This is all hairy, and I apologize. But I think that the result as it stands might be a bit off. AFAICT, the workflows look like:

* If we are using the new optimization:
    1. Don't actually build a requirements-only PEX, and instead hold onto the requirements which would have been used
    2. When actually creating a PEX containing user code, embed the thirdparty requirements
* If we are not using the optimization:
    1. Build a requirements-only PEX, and put it on the pex-path of another PEX containing user code.

The differentiation between "a requirements PEX" (which contains only thirdparty requirements) and the PEX for user code was intentional (although how important it is in practice is unclear), because thirdparty requirements and your code's transitive thirdparty deps change much less frequently than anything else.

So I think that you'll actually want to preserve that aspect, by adjusting the optimization to always create a `RequirementsPex`, but to do so directly from the Lockfile, rather than from a "repository PEX". That should (AFAICT?) avoid the need for the new type at all, because [the existing `RequirementsPex` creation code](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/8d53a4447513de121153a301f737f40679bc6eaa/src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_from_targets.py#L624-L625) can be adjusted directly.

This calculates the time spent in the current session, and it's fine for that purpose.
However this time was being used to calculate the completion percentage and it's the reason why users are complaining of spending 50 minutes in the resource without being able to complete it.
If the user reads a pdf or see some html5 content in different days because he does not complete the whole time in a row, using totalTime will make the user unable to get the completion percentage if it's restarted everytime he begins because the previous spent time is not taken into account to calculate the completion percentage.

For example, in a pdf with 40 pages, completion time will be 40 x 30 = 1200 seconds. If the user reads 30 pages spending 15 minutes, and returns the next day to continue, all the previous time is discarded if we don't use the previous time spent (avaialable in the vuex getter) to calculate the percentage, so he might be reading the document forever without getting the completion time if he never spends those 1200 seconds (20 min) in a single session.

execute_sensor_iteration_loop is called exactly once in the sensor daemon (SensorDaemon is not an IntervalDaemon). So this is already getting instantiated exactly once in the sensor daemon

unless you mean sharing the executor across multiple daemons (scheduler/sensor/run queue/backfill/etc.), which is an interesting idea with some tradeoffs.

The pros are that we could have one executor with the ideal number of threads for the process, and when one daemon isn't doing much the other daemons would be able to get full utilization out of the threadpool

The cons are that there would be a risk of all other daemons falling behind when one daemon kicks off a bunch of threads (i'm imagining the run queue daemon will get a threadpool soon too - so imagining a bunch of runs getting launched and the sensor daemons falling behind and timing out)

- `progress` is a prop, and its value is coming from the [`contentProgress` prop in the `LearningActivityBar` component ](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/release-v0.15.x/kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/views/LearningActivityBar.vue#L181)

> My biggest question/concern around this PR is the complexity of this new contract between providers and the engine. It feels like correctly computing these is going to be "hard" for a new provider.

For what it's worth, it is not necessary for the provider to compute these if it can guarantee that the only diffs it sees are detectable by a simple diff of the old inputs against the new inputs (or the new inputs against the old state). If you can think of a better way to do this for providers that don't have this characteristic (e.g. TF providers), I am open to it.

> Have we verified that we can do this completely correctly for at least Terraform-based providers and Kubernetes providers?

For TF providers, yes. We should be able to do this for k8s as well by deriving the diff from a merge patch.

> Do we have any example of doing this for a non-schematized provider? That is - for someone brining up a raw provider from scratch backed by e.g. a simple REST API - in practice how much net complexity does this add?
> ...
> Should we be thinking about a schema model for native Pulumi providers that (among other things) gives this "for free"? Something that allows a higher-level definition of new Pulumi providers (at least in Go)?

I believe that these two things are so closely related that they really form one point. I do think that we should consider investing in some sort of schema model for native Pulumi providers, yes. https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-openfaas/blob/master/pkg/provider/provider.go#L109-L119 is an example of one experimental approach using Go struct tags.

In a commercial environment, the number of multicast groups may be much larger - Thread 1.2 Commercial is looking to handling hundreds of multicast groups, for example.

I agree that having a dedicated multicast address table for reports will be more efficient from a processing perspective. I am not so sure it saves on memory because we already allocate 3 addresses per child.

For unicast addresses, we have similar processing overhead:

1. When forwarding IPv6 packets, we need to scan potentially hundreds of IPv6 addresses to determine which child.

1. When performing duplicate address detection, we need to scan potentially hundreds of IPv6 addresses to ensure uniqueness across all children.

The main backwards compatibility that we want to guard against is wire/format compatibility, backwards compat with compilation is nice, but not strictly necessary.  Wire compatibility means: the size and format of bytes in the message cannot change, for bit-field messages we can add new bit-fields and deprecate old ones and in some cases we can change the interpretation of bytes (e.g. from unsignged to signed) but these cases need to be considered carefully.

- If we use a simple `uint8_t`/`uint16_t`/`uint32_t` as  bitflags, compilers can optimize it well.

```suggestion
### Generic container input types are not always preserved

In Pydantic V1 we made great efforts to preserve the types of all field inputs, even when they were proper subtypes of the field annotations.

For example, given the annotation `Mapping[str, int]` if you passed in a `collection.Counter()` you'd get a `collection.Counter()` as the value.

Due to the complexity and performance implications of supporting this while also applying validation to the contained values, in Pydantic V2 we no longer promise that we will always preserve the input type (although we still try to where possible). Instead, we now only promise that the post-validation value for a field will match its annotation.

In the case of `Mapping[str, int]` for example, if you pass in a `collection.Counter()`, the validated value might just be a plain Python dict. (Note that we still promise the post-validation value will be a valid `Mapping`.)

If you want the output type to be a specific type, consider annotating it as such, or add a `mode='wrap'` validator function that ensures the final result of validation will have the type you want.
```

I think without a bit more detail about the intent, this section may sound a bit disturbing to users. I took what you wrote and just tried to soften it a bit, saying:
* It only applies to generic containers (I think this is true, at least in intent..)
* We still view it as better to preserve the input type where possible, just, not doing that under all circumstances has benefits, and we want to take advantage of those
* You can implement more carefully type-preserving behavior yourself using custom validators.

Feel free to take/leave any of the suggested changes.

- otTcpConnectionEstablished --- triggered when TCP is connected (i.e. enter `ESTABLISHED` state)

- otTcpClosed --- triggered when the TCP is closed (enters `CLOSED` state) for any reason

Since a TCP socket should enter `CLOSED` state eventually, `otTcpClosed` provides a convenient place for ALP to reuse or release the socket.

Ok thanks for the clarification. So the idea is to block any other tasks (radio, non-radio, all) from executing while the Tx is ongoing. This seems difficult to achieve, because other processes might register timers for an earlier time, and so these other processes may still get executed earlier than the time this timer is set for.

The otPlatAlarmMicroStartAt() only adds a time to the timer, leaving other times that were already set in place. When all tasks that were pending have run, control is passed back to OTNS in platform-sim.c by a blocking wait on the UDP socket which is the real moment the process blocks. To really simulate the radio Tx blocking every other task, there would need to be some change in platform-sim.c and/or the CLI app to suspend task processing in the main loop while Tx is still ongoing.  I also wonder if other radios than CC2538 do the same? Because it's a rather 'nasty' way of breaking the OT task system - I can check maybe at some point with OT authors about the intended design here.

In FontLab 7, when you assign a special tag to the target glyph, it replaces the source glyph under specified conditions:

```
[srcglyph]~cond1[&cond2][&cond3...]
```

if `srcglyph` is absent, the current glyph’s name without a suffix is used.

Each cond is a 2-letter (internal FL) axis tag with bounds, like `wt>500` or `340>wd` or `100<op<600` etc. — and they're concatenated with `&`.

If I need 'OR', I add a second special tag. 

I know there is some redundancy in this, but I’m genuinely curious if there are conditionsets that involve both OR and AND which cannot be expressed as a list of 'AND' conditionsets.

I see the point. I checked the code, we already eliminated `atomic_fence` from the hottest paths, so even inefficient register allocation does not seem to be the issue. Hence, let's go with your solution because it is indifferent for Intel-based CPU.

> I can limit the asm code to gcc versions prior to 11 if you prefer, though I see no harm in leaving it enabled unconditionally. 

The main harm is two versions of the code that can become different over the time and require multiple configurations to test them. Moreover, the assembler limits compiler capabilities to optimize nearby code and generate better code for future CPUs (what if someone invents one more good instruction?). In addition, memory model is really complex topic and it is better to have implementation as simple as possible to avoid unforeseen issues and apply such tools like Thread Sanitizer to check the correctness but Thread Sanitizer does not understand assembler. Unfortunately, it does not understand standalone barriers but there are some attempts (mentioned in https://github.com/google/sanitizers/issues/1415) to add them. Therefore, I prefer to keep workarounds limited and rely on generic coded as well tested and reasonably performant solutions.

TLDR; So did some investigation + discussion with Kashif, and found the following things:
- The `socket_timeout` and `socket_timeout_interval` _should_ be the same value. - `block_with_timeout` is unnecessary/redundanct, so we've removed it.
- for sync, the socket timeout is passed in when creating the connection and acts as the read/write timeout for both websocket and ssl transport.
- since the connect timeout has a different purpose, I've added a separate config/kwarg back to SSLTransport. We can expose this at some point in the future if we need to, but it is set to 1 by default for now.
- sync ws doesn't seem to take a separate timeout for connecting
- for async, read/write timeouts on both ssl and websocket are controlled by asyncio.wait_for(), which is where we pass in the socket_timeout.
- For async ssl, there is no separate connect_timeout.
- For async ws, we pass in a connect_timeout when creating the connection. If we don't, it's 10 secs by default.

More info on block_with_timeout:
In sync, this socket_timeout value is passed into connection.listen(...) all the way through to [`_read_frame`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/09035248a8e9fa27ea07cd4cbcdde2f1b6efdcfc/sdk/servicebus/azure-servicebus/azure/servicebus/_pyamqp/_connection.py#L246) and [`_send_frame`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/09035248a8e9fa27ea07cd4cbcdde2f1b6efdcfc/sdk/servicebus/azure-servicebus/azure/servicebus/_pyamqp/_connection.py#L273). Inside those methods, if a timeout value _has_ been passed in, then it calls the `[block_with_timeout](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/09035248a8e9fa27ea07cd4cbcdde2f1b6efdcfc/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/_transport.py#L205)` method on the transport. However, we found that this method is not used/useful with the way that we setup our socket. The way that we currently do this by just calling send/receive_frame, it [_already_ blocks with timeout b/c we call `sock.settimeout()` (which is a blocking call)](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/09035248a8e9fa27ea07cd4cbcdde2f1b6efdcfc/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/_transport.py#L348) when we _init_socket. That method was [used before when we used OS timeouts, rather than socket timeouts](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/09035248a8e9fa27ea07cd4cbcdde2f1b6efdcfc/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/_transport.py#L335), which I believe were non-blocking by default.

It's because of the custom argparse extension which tries to build ReST from certain text patterns used in the argparse help texts:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/3309ed0620fa4aedeee218a48996d8284a90bf4b/docs/ext_argparse.py#L22
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/3309ed0620fa4aedeee218a48996d8284a90bf4b/docs/ext_argparse.py#L72-L73

Text indented by at least 2 spaces gets turned into a literal-block which then gets highlighted by Sphinx automatically when there's no block type specified:
https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/basics.html#literal-blocks

There are a couple of other examples in the docs where it's parsing it incorrectly, but we can't use full ReST syntax in the argparse help texts because of the `--help` output and the man page, so it's a bit difficult to fix this.

> I don't know what other channels are usually used to deliver a token, apart from email or SMS.

This is a good logical fallacy to become familiar with: it's sometimes called proof by lack of imagination.

This is a framework. There are an infinite number of devices that might be written and the only rule is that they must inherit from `Device`. If you're thinking just in terms of the specific devices you know about or imagine, then you're thinking too small. The question is what's the right thing to do for every device that has ever or will ever be written.

Mixins and things like `SideChannelDevice` are convenience classes that help people write standard categories of devices in standard ways. But we can never imagine all possibilities, so any device authors are free to ignore the helpers and start from scratch. That's why `generate_token` and `verify_is_allowed` are on the root `Device` class: they don't apply to *every* device, but they could apply to *any* device.
